lines.text: Alright, we're midnight. {{ELAPSED}} in and the moon is *full* out there — you can see everything tonight, folks. Everything. So let's burn it down proper. NOFX. 'Linoleum.' Off Punk in Drublic. 1994.

Fat Mike, Erik Sandin, Eric Melvin — these three have been doing this since 1983, Fairfax High School in LA. Never signed to a major. Never took the machine. And that 'Leave It Alone' video? They made it, looked at it, and said 'MTV can fuck off.' I respect that kind of spite.

Punk in Drublic hit number twelve on the Heatseekers chart and went gold six years later — not because they chased it, but because they didn't. That's the Purge energy right there. Do it your way or don't do it.

This record came after the Vance shuffle, after the first Purge years were normalizing. NOFX was already political — racism, middle-class collapse, they were on it. 'Linoleum' is just *living* with it. Work, sleep, repeat. The spice must flow, as they say, and the rent must be paid.
Green Day. 'Basket Case.' Dookie. 1994. Same year. Same month, practically.

Billie Joe Armstrong, Mike Dirnt — Bay Area kids, both of them broken early. Armstrong's father died when he was ten. Dirnt's mother was a heroin addict. They met at 924 Gilman Street in Berkeley and decided to build something that couldn't be broken further because it was already in pieces.

Dookie was recorded in three weeks at Fantasy Studios. Three weeks. They captured the whole anxiety, the boredom, the masturbation, the mass murder — all of it. Billie was writing about what was actually *happening* in his head. Sold twenty million copies worldwide. Twenty million people realized they weren't alone in their anxiety spiral.
This song — 'Basket Case' — it's about panic. About feeling like you're losing it and there's nobody to call because everyone's too busy. Sound familiar? That's been Purge night for 140 years.
The Descendents. 'Suburban Home.' Milo Goes to College. 1982. And Milo — his actual name is Milo Aukerman — he *did* go to UC San Diego. That's not a song title. That's a mission statement.

Manhattan Beach, California. Frank Navetta, Tony Lombardo, Bill Stevenson — they started as power pop, surf punk. Then Milo came in and they became melodic hardcore. But here's the thing: they sang about love, food, rejection, girls. The stuff actually mattered. Not just rage.

A classmate named Rodger Deuerlein used to draw comics of Milo as the class nerd. Milo just *kept going*. The caricature ended up on album covers. Turned the taunting into iconography. That's what you do when you're a real punk — you own what they mock you for.

And Frank Navetta — after I Don't Want to Grow Up, he left the band to become a *fisherman* in Oregon. A fisherman. That's the most punk rock retirement I've ever heard. He chose the ocean over the road.

tracks.title: And We Thought Nation States Were A Bad Idea

lines.text: Propagandhi. 'And We Thought Nation States Were A Bad Idea.' Potemkin City Limits. 2005. Canadian. Winnipeg. And if you know anything about Propagandhi, you know they don't fuck around with the politics.

Chris Hannah, Jord Samolesky — they came out of the Manitoba DIY scene. Fat Mike signed them to Fat Wreck Chords in '92 after they opened for NOFX. And they were *immediately* controversial because they came out pro-feminism, pro-gay rights in a scene that was still soaked in sexism and homophobia. They made people uncomfortable on purpose.

Potemkin City Limits is where they went full anarchist. Global politics, corporate imperialism, environmental collapse — they're singing about the systems that built the Purge. And they're doing it with venom. Ramsey Kanaan from AK Press even voices the oil corporation on this record. That's cross-pollination of resistance.

Nation states. Yeah. They were right. Here we are in 2169 and the thing that's killing us is the same architecture that was killing us in 2005. Propagandhi saw it coming. They're still here. Still angry.

tracks.title: Rocking Robin
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'Rocking Robin.' 1997. A cover band. A punk cover band. Built by Fat Mike, Chris Shiflett, Joey Cape, Spike Slawson — guys from NOFX, Lagwagon, No Use for a Name. They did this in their *off-time*.

The Gimmes take pop songs, 60s and 70s hits, show tunes, country, R&B — whatever — and they punk the *shit* out of them. Rapid-fire, humorous, completely serious underneath it. They recorded at One Week Records in San Francisco, produced by Ryan Greene. Three-week turn-around. Same as Dookie.

And the thing I love about the Gimmes is that they wear costumes — matching, ridiculous costumes. Cowboy outfits. Drag. Because they know that if you're going to subvert something, you have to look like you're having fun doing it. That's the secret the Vance regime never figured out: you can't kill what's already laughing at you.
'Rocking Robin' — they turned a 1950s novelty song into a punk anthem. That's alchemy, man. That's what covers do right — they *resurrect*.

Dead Milkmen. 'Bitchin' Camaro.' Big Lizard in My Backyard. 1985. Philadelphia. Rodney Anonymous, Joe Jack Talcum, Dave Blood, Dean Clean — that's the core. And the song title comes from a monologue intro they improvise every single night. Never the same twice.

The Milkmen started as a *home-recording project* in Chester County — Genaro's bedroom, basically. He and a friend created a fictional band before they were actually a band. The name came from Toni Morrison — Milkman Dead from Song of Solomon. They were building mythology out of nothing.

This song — it became a college radio phenomenon. MTV rotation. And it stayed in rotation because of that improvised dialogue. Every time they play it, it's different. That's punk rock's secret weapon: you can't commodify what's never the same twice.

The Dead Milkmen proved you could be jangly, satirical, absurdist — you could sing about jellyfish afterlifes and UFO conspiracies and toxic waste — and still be *dangerous*. Humor is a blade. They swung it with thick Philadelphia accents and nobody saw it coming.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide cumulative at 00:00: 82 kills YoY. East Side holding its heat. Capitol Square breach cleared at 21:40 — 6 casualties in security perimeter.
Full moon visibility 10+ miles. State Street Ruins 23:00–00:30 coordinated sweep: 14 kills in 90-minute window. Highest single-event sequence so far.
Cold front arriving 01:00–01:30. Temperature dropping to 29F. Wind gusting NW 18 mph. Caroline was right about the timing. She will mention this.
